sports_feed._note: Refreshed by the refresh-sports-feed scheduled task. Each item is {headline, url, source, published (ISO8601), snark}. Snark voice: Barstool-adjacent guy-at-the-bar, crass, horny, cocky, zero filler. One sentence. No slurs, no punching down at specific real people in a harmful way. Punch sideways (at media, front offices, rival fanbases) and up (at ownership). Keep it Boston.

items.headline: Red Sox open series at Cleveland, Samaniego opener before Bello follows
items.snark: Opener strategy is back because Breslow doesn't trust his starters to give him six outs without hemorrhaging runs.

items.headline: Willson Contreras extends hitting streak to 11 games, tied for longest in MLB
items.snark: Only guy on the roster who remembered how to swing a bat, turns out the Cubs knew what they were doing.

items.headline: Sox 1 and 7 and 1 in series play at Fenway, dropped another homestand Thursday
items.snark: Fenway is supposed to be a fortress, instead it's a hospice where opposing pitchers rehab their ERAs.

items.headline: Braves hammer Red Sox 10 to 2, Greg Weissert blows up again in sixth inning
items.snark: Weissert serving up meatballs like it's Thanksgiving and the bullpen is the family that won't stop asking for seconds.

items.headline: Patriots and Eagles reportedly not particularly close on A.J. Brown trade deal
items.snark: Not particularly close is NFL insider speak for the Eagles want a first and the Pats offered a Dunkin gift card.

items.headline: Will Campbell returns to left tackle after rough Super Bowl vs Seahawks
items.snark: Campbell got cooked in February, now he's back at LT because depth charts don't care about your trauma.

ranked_opportunities.scqa: The SAR Buyer Side
ranked_opportunities.verdict: EnduroSat is a Bulgarian software-flexible satellite manufacturer at a US-build inflection: $147M raised in six months (Founders Fund, Riot, Google Ventures), new EnduroSat USA CEO Rusty Thomas (former DARPA Project Blackjack) tripling Denver from 5 to 15+. The March 2026 MetaSensing X-band SAR partnership (PhoeniX, StarSAR-X on FRAME, ~0.125m resolution) is the most commercially interesting move and a direct match to Paul's KSAT decade selling NRT SAR services into oil and gas, maritime, and environmental compliance. Tier 2, pursue with caveats; the early-conversation question is whether this seat is commercial-services-led or pulled toward the DC gov-pipeline build (Shield Space, SDA-supplier outreach).
ranked_opportunities.doc_note: Full deep dive completed 2026-05-15. Top SCQA: The SAR Buyer Side (91.5/100). Recommended resume: EO/Maritime (primary), Ecosystem/Platform (alternate). Open commercial-vs-gov scoping question with Rusty Thomas before applying.

ranked_opportunities.role: Senior Director, Subscription & Data Solutions @ LevelTen Energy | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role requires deep domain experience in the renewable energy PPA market that Paul does not possess. The JD explicitly requires "10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem" and "Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors." Paul's 15 years of commercial infrastructure experience is in satellite monitoring (oil & gas, fishing surveillance, maritime), not renewable energy transactions. While the role structure, commercial-product bridge, subscription packaging, data monetization strategy, maps beautifully to Paul's skills from GFW (building commercial licensing for an open platform, multi-stakeholder deal architecture), the sector mismatch is a hard blocker. RF capped at 5 because the responsibilities fit Paul's profile but the required domain does not. IL capped at 4 because Paul cannot credibly claim the named renewable energy market experience or customer base. The role is well-compensated and the stage is interesting (post-PMF data platform scaling subscriptions), but Paul would need to explain a full sector pivot from space/maritime to renewable energy, and the JD signals they want someone who has "been living and breathing this market for years."

blockers.type: Hard requirement: 10+ years renewable energy market experience
blockers.detail: JD requires '10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem.' Paul has 15 years in commercial space/satellite sector (KSAT 2011-2021, GFW 2021-2026) but not in renewable energy PPAs, energy procurement, or the utility/developer ecosystem that LevelTen serves. His infrastructure work was water/wastewater utility security (PA Consulting) and satellite monitoring, not renewable energy transactions.

blockers.type: Hard requirement: Significant commercial/sales experience selling to energy buyers, developers, utilities, advisors
blockers.detail: JD requires 'Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors and understand how deals get done.' Paul's sales experience is in satellite monitoring services to oil & gas, fishing surveillance, and maritime clients. He has not sold to renewable energy buyers, PPAs, or clean energy developers.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a carbon credit sales role at an Asia-focused carbon project platform. While the mission aligns strongly with Paul's climate/sustainability focus and the entrepreneurial framing is appealing, the core work is enterprise carbon credit sales ,  managing pipeline, closing deals, forecasting ,  not building a commercial function or creating demand in a new market. The JD explicitly requires "7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management" and describes the role as "responsible for the entire carbon credits sales cycle." Paul's KSAT and GFW experience demonstrates commercial development and multi-stakeholder deal architecture, but he is not a quota-carrying enterprise seller. RF capped at 5 because the role shape does not match Paul's market-builder identity. IL at 5 because Paul has relevant carbon/climate sector knowledge (GFW commercial licensing, KSAT EO for environmental compliance) but lacks the named "carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." SA scores 6 because Rubicon is described as expanding and the role is "newly added due to business expansion," but the role itself is about selling an existing product (carbon credits) to enterprises, not building the commercial function. SPM scores 7 because multi-stakeholder carbon credit deals and navigating complex corporate buyers fits Paul's pattern, even though the role itself is misaligned. Singapore preference is a soft geographic blocker.

blockers.detail: Role has 'strong preference for candidates based in Singapore.' Paul is based in Taos, NM. While location is described as 'flexible,' the preference signal creates friction.
Role is 'entire carbon credits sales cycle' including 'managing pipeline, executing account strategies' ,  this is quota-carrying enterprise sales, not market-building.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role fails on three hard constraints. First, geographic: Singapore-based with strong preference for local candidates; Paul is in Taos and Singapore relocation is a substantial ask. Second, the JD explicitly requires "10, 15+ years of relevant client-facing deal making experience with 5-7 years+ in carbon markets, climate finance, project investing" ,  Paul has 5 years total in the climate/carbon space (GFW 2021-2026), not 5-7+ in carbon markets specifically. Third, the role demands "deep expertise in carbon markets, including project pathways, quality guardrails, offtake structures, supply and demand dynamics" and "significant experience originating, structuring, negotiating, and executing complex multi-million dollar commercial transactions, with a focus on environmental commodity transactions." Paul built a commercial licensing program at GFW; he did not invest in carbon projects, structure offtakes, or negotiate carbon credit purchase agreements. RF capped at 5 (role involves deal-making and multi-stakeholder coordination but in a domain Paul lacks depth), IL capped at 4 (experience gap is explicit and quantified), GC capped at 2 (Singapore residency preference is explicit). SPM scores 8 because the multi-stakeholder deal architecture, portfolio construction, and market-building patterns are strongly present, but that does not overcome the hard blockers.

blockers.detail: Role based in Singapore with strong preference for Singapore-based candidates. Paul is based in Taos, NM and while open to relocation, Singapore represents a significant geographic and cultural distance.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role is enterprise carbon credit sales in Asia, a market Paul has never sold in, from a geography where he is not based. The **hard requirement cap fires on RF and IL**: the JD states "At least 7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management, or commercial origination, preferably involving complex solutions for large corporate customers" and "Carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." Paul meets the enterprise sales structure and complexity, but he has zero carbon market sales experience and zero Asia commercial network. The "strong preference for candidates based in Singapore" is not a residency requirement, but it signals a strong soft blocker. **RF = 5**: Paul has deep consultative enterprise sales experience (KSAT, GFW), C-level engagement, and complex solution sales, but this is carbon credits, a market he has never sold in. **IL = 4**: Paul's network is EO/maritime/IUU fishing, not carbon or Asia enterprise buyers. **SA = 6**: Rubicon is described as "fast-evolving," "newly added due to business expansion," but it is NOT a greenfield commercial function, it's scaling an existing carbon sales engine. **SPM = 7**: Multi-stakeholder coordination, consultative selling to corporates with sustainability mandates, and building markets in emerging regulatory environments all pattern-match Paul's career, but this is sales execution in an existing market, not market creation. **GC = 3**: Singapore preference is a major friction point. Total weighted = 50.5, rounded to 51. This is pursue_with_caveats: the strategic patterns fit, but the domain and geography gaps are material.

blockers.detail: Role strongly prefers Singapore-based candidates. Paul is US-based in Taos, NM. While the JD says 'location is flexible,' the strong preference for Singapore creates friction for a US-based candidate.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: Ceres AI (formerly Ceres Imaging) does precision agriculture analytics using aerial/satellite imagery. This role targets agricultural capital markets: lenders, insurers, and banks pricing crop risk. The AXA/British Marine work is structurally analogous (satellite data used to price insurance exposure) but this is an IC quota-carrying AE role, not commercial leadership. watch_list.why: Full-stack biochar platform, agribusiness deployment model, emerging markets focus. YC-backed. Practical carbon removal at scale.
watch_list.roast: Terraton is a San Francisco biochar company founded by two people who used to work at Google and they are deploying biochar facilities in emerging markets and generating carbon credits and the entire pitch is we will make carbon removal a product not a science experiment. Charm Industrial is doing the same thing with biomass liquids. Varaha is doing it in India with cost discipline and verified credits. CarbonZero is doing it with 16-year-old founders and thirty thousand ton kilns. Everyone in the biochar space is racing to prove the unit economics work before the voluntary carbon market figures out which removal pathways are real and which ones are accounting. The companies that survive are the ones that have corporate offtake agreements and third-party verification and a path to profit before the next market correction. Terraton has the full-stack model and the agribusiness partnerships and the YC brand which gets you meetings but does not close deals. What they need is someone who has spent fifteen years explaining to CFOs why paying for something invisible today prevents a much larger visible cost in three years. Someone who knows how to structure a pilot so the customer feels like they are setting a standard not taking a risk. Someone who understands that the sale is not the biochar it is the compliance narrative and the stakeholder choreography and the regulatory air cover. You have done this in three sectors and two continents. The question is whether you want to do it again for a technology that might actually matter or whether you are tired of being the person in the room who has to explain why the thing that makes sense is also the thing nobody wants to be first to buy.

watch_list.why: French government multi-year contract for 10-satellite constellation, multi-sensor architecture, sovereign EO capability. Paul already applied but worth a roast.
watch_list.roast: Wait, you already sent them a resume? Of course you did. Because when a company announces a multi-year French government contract worth tens of millions of euros to deploy 10 satellites with optical, thermal infrared, hyperspectral, and RF sensors all on one bus, your Pavlovian response is to write a cover letter. Loft is doing the thing you spent a decade at KSAT trying to explain to oil majors: you can put more than one sensor on a satellite and get answers instead of just pictures. They are calling it AI-enabled space infrastructure. You are calling it Tuesday. The program is sovereign, which means the sales cycle is over before it started, but the operational build-out is just beginning. If they had hired you in March, you would be in Paris right now explaining to CNES why the fourth satellite needs a different orbit. Instead, you are in Taos refreshing this dashboard. First launch is Q4 2026. By then you will be somewhere else, watching their press release, wondering if they even opened your email.

watch_list.why: Seven GRUS-3 satellites launching 2026, 2.2m resolution, existing constellation serving 30+ countries, AxelGlobe commercial platform strong fit for Paul's EO data licensing background.
watch_list.roast: Japanese microsatellite company launching seven GRUS-3 satellites in 2026 to expand their constellation past ten birds. Each one is 150kg, 2.2-meter resolution, seven bands including coastal blue and red edge, which is more color options than you have had in your dating life since the divorce. Axelspace has been at this since 2008, which makes them older than New Space but younger than your MBA. They serve government and commercial customers in more than 30 countries, which sounds impressive until you realize that KSAT served more than that by 2015 and you were the one who built half the deck. Their AxelGlobe platform sells Earth observation data for precision agriculture, forest monitoring, and financial product development, which is exactly the commercial licensing model you built at GFW except theirs makes money and yours got defunded. The company is in Tokyo. You are in Taos. The timezone makes Tromso look convenient. But they have the data flow, the manufacturing cadence, and the customer base. You know how to sell this product because you have sold worse versions of it to harder buyers. The question is whether they know they need you, or whether they think the satellites do the work themselves.

watch_list.why: Spanish EO startup, $130M Series B (April 2026), L3Harris sensor partnership, building constellation ground-up for on-board ML inference. Direct KSAT/GFW overlap.
watch_list.roast: Xoople just closed a $130 million Series B in April to build AI-native Earth observation satellites out of Madrid with a manufacturing partnership with L3Harris, which means they convinced defense contractors and European climate VCs that the next wave of EO is not about better cameras but about putting the neural net on the bird so the data gets smart before it hits the ground. They are Spanish, well-funded, and explicitly positioning against the legacy players like Planet and Maxar who were not built for AI inference at the edge. The thesis is good. The capital is real. The risk is that they are pre-revenue or close to it, and you would be walking in to build the commercial function while the eng team is still building the satellites. That is the job you like, but it is also the job with the highest fragility because if the constellation does not work the way the pitch deck says it works, no amount of commercial genius will save the ARR target. You have done the multi-stakeholder sale, the regulatory precedent play, the long cycle enterprise deal with super majors and governments. Xoople needs that, but they need it in Brussels, not Houston, and they need you to sell a product that does not exist yet to buyers who are not sure they need it. The L3Harris partnership is the validation that matters. If a defense prime is willing to build your sensors, someone believes the unit economics work. Whether that someone is right is a 2027 problem. Whether you want to move to Madrid or commute from Taos is a 2026 one.

watch_list.why: European startup (€3.5M March 2026), synthetic satellite data generation for training EO models. Deforestation, mining, agriculture focus. NovaTerra Brazil partnership, GeoTerra Africa.
watch_list.roast: Another Earth is generating fake satellite imagery to train real AI models and somehow this is a legitimate business in 2026. They raised €3.5M in March to scale their Synthetic Data Engine, which produces labeled EO datasets without actually launching anything into orbit. If you squint, this is the same problem you hit at GFW when you needed Planet imagery for the Audacious grant but the imagery was not where you needed it or when you needed it or at the price you needed it. Synthetic data does not solve the revisit problem but it solves the cost problem and the labeling problem, which is why organizations training models care. Another Earth is working in Brazil and Sub-Saharan Africa on deforestation monitoring, mining site oversight, and agricultural risk. These are exactly the verticals where you know the buyers, the use cases, and the reasons the status quo does not work. You negotiated the Planet deal that put 1.2 billion hectares under daily surveillance. You know what it costs to acquire real imagery at scale and you know what the processing pipeline looks like on the back end. Another Earth is not trying to replace real satellites. They are trying to make the data cheaper and faster to use for organizations that cannot afford a Planet contract or do not need wall-to-wall coverage. The customer is different but the pain point is identical. You also know how to sell into environmental monitoring programs backed by multilateral funding, which is exactly where Another Earth is operating. This is not a perfect fit but it is adjacent enough that a conversation would be worth having. Worst case, you learn how synthetic data economics actually work. Best case, they need someone who has licensed real EO data at scale and can tell them where the synthetic version actually matters.

watch_list.why: Thermal infrared EO constellation, agriculture/water stress monitoring, founded 2017, predictive analytics. Addresses gap KSAT/Planet do not cover well.
watch_list.roast: Hydrosat is flying thermal infrared satellites to measure water stress and you spent ten years at KSAT selling optical and SAR to clients who constantly asked if you could see temperature. The answer was always no, or not well, or not at the resolution that mattered for precision agriculture or drought monitoring. Hydrosat exists because thermal EO has been the missing middle of the remote sensing stack for two decades. Everyone knows it matters. Almost no one has built a commercial constellation that works. They were founded in 2017 so they are not a seed-stage science project. They have launched, they have customers, and they are scaling. The use cases are agriculture, water management, and climate risk, which means the buyer profile overlaps with everyone you have sold to. You know how to walk into an agriculture ministry or a conservation program and explain why daily revisit matters. You know how to explain why free data from Landsat or Sentinel is not sufficient when the client needs sub-field resolution and a three-day latency guarantee. Hydrosat is not trying to replace Planet or Maxar. They are filling the gap those companies do not cover, which is exactly the kind of market positioning you understand. The GFW experience is relevant here because you built a licensing program for an organization that was giving data away for free, and you had to identify the use cases where paid access actually made sense. Hydrosat has the opposite problem. They have unique data and they need to find the customers who will pay for it before the use case is fully proven. That is the pilot-to-precedent engine you ran at KSAT for a decade. This is not a moonshot. This is a company with satellites in orbit, a defined market, and a commercial model that needs someone who knows how to build enterprise relationships in environmental data. You are that person. Go talk to them.

watch_list.why: Asia-Pacific methane detection, AI-driven satellite analytics, WEF backing for MRV integration into financial frameworks
watch_list.roast: Seoul-based satellite play running methane detection for Asia-Pacific governments who just realized self-reported inventories mean nothing and satellite truth hurts. They caught the WEF tailwind on methane MRV right as the EU turned monitoring into a compliance hammer and the US methane fee hit $900/ton in 2024, climbing to $1500 by next year. NARA is selling the thing KSAT built a market for a decade ago but for the gas nobody wanted to talk about because you cannot see it and it breaks models. Paul spent years explaining why oil majors should pay for what they cannot touch, and methane is the same problem at higher stakes because it warms 80x faster than CO2 over 20 years and regulators finally care. The pitch is continuous satellite monitoring plus AI analytics that turn opacity into enforcement, which is the only model that scales when you are trying to move an entire industrial sector that would rather not know. NARA is early, the Asia-Pacific regulatory build is just starting, and they need someone who has sold the invisible before and knows the buyers who will pay are the ones who legally cannot afford not to. Remote first, Korea time zone overlap, probably Series A soon.

watch_list.why: No-code satellite EO analytics for nature-based carbon MRV, compliance-grade monitoring for Article 6 and CORSIA, building market infrastructure
watch_list.roast: UK-based platform trying to make satellite EO accessible to the carbon market people who do not want to learn Python or hire a remote sensing PhD just to prove their reforestation project did not burn down. They are selling continuous monitoring for nature-based solutions at exactly the moment when Article 6 and CORSIA turned voluntary offsets into compliance assets and buyers realized infrequent site visits by consultants with clipboards will not survive a financial audit. Paul spent a career translating satellite data into commercial arguments for people who do not care how SAR works, they just want to know if the thing they paid for is still there, and Earth Blox built the dashboard that does that without requiring a PhD. The 2026 carbon market is finally past jargon-heavy voluntary nonsense and into data-backed responsibility, which means the MRV infrastructure companies are the new picks and shovels, not the project developers. The model is proactive oversight using satellite monitoring to de-risk compliance-grade credits under Singapore Article 6 contracts and Brazilian SBCE forestry rules, both of which just came online this year. Remote-first, London HQ, probably raising growth round soon because every NbS project developer and corporate buyer will need this or something like it before 2027. The boring infrastructure bet that makes the market work, which is exactly where Paul should be.

watch_list.why: Farmer-first soil carbon MRV, practice-based + satellite verification, pragmatic middle ground between measurement cost and credibility
watch_list.roast: Danish agtech trying to solve the problem child of carbon markets, which is soil carbon, because you cannot see it, measuring it costs more than the credit is worth, and pure practice-based estimates have zero credibility with anyone holding a compliance budget. Agreena built a hybrid model that tracks regenerative practices, verifies implementation through satellite imagery, and quantifies carbon benefits through modeling that splits the difference between perfect measurement and faith-based accounting. They monetize farmer behavior change instead of waiting for soil samples that take six months and cost $80 each, which is the only way soil carbon scales past boutique projects in Iowa. The model works because institutional buyers will pay for credible-enough if the methodology is transparent and the monitoring is continuous, which is exactly the trade GFW made when Paul sold the ASN and Planet deals, you are not selling perfection, you are selling structured verification that stands behind the number. Agreena has traction with European agribusinesses who face EUDR and CSRD reporting, and the US farm belt is starting to care because regen ag subsidies and crop insurance discounts are starting to price in carbon. Remote-first, Copenhagen-based, Series B-ish stage, probably needs a partnerships lead who understands how to build the commercial licensing program on top of the MRV platform and structure deals with food companies and insurers who have never bought a carbon credit before. Paul has done this twice.

watch_list.why: First commercial OptoSAR satellite. India private space. All-weather imaging domain fit.
watch_list.roast: Bengaluru startup just launched Mission Drishti, their 190kg OptoSAR bird that fuses electro-optical and synthetic aperture radar on a single platform. First in the world, they say. Launched May 3 on a Falcon 9 rideshare out of Vandenberg, right alongside the South Korean government payload and 43 other hitchhikers. You spent a decade at KSAT selling the value proposition of SAR to oil majors who did not want to hear it in month six of a two-year sales cycle. GalaxEye's pitch is the same fight but faster: all-weather, day-night, cloud-penetrating imagery for buyers who are tired of optical satellites going dark every time the monsoon rolls in. Raised $18.8M from Infosys and others. Partnered with NewSpace India Limited for global distribution, which means they understood the government-as-channel play before they had revenue. The technology is legitimately novel. The market is every vertical you already know. The problem is GalaxEye is targeting a gap in Earth observation you have lived in since Tromso, and they are doing it in a geography where your KSAT rolodex does not translate but your product-market-fit instinct absolutely does. They need someone who has sat across the table from ExxonMobil and Shell and knows that the people who buy SAR are not the people who buy optical, and the contract structures reflect that reality. If they grow past seed stage, this is the exact commercial build you have done twice.

watch_list.why: Singapore-based. AI-driven hyperspectral flood/wildfire detection. Adaptation vertical heating up.
watch_list.roast: Singapore startup doing AI-powered Earth observation with hyperspectral imaging and satellite data to detect floods, wildfires, and ecosystem shifts. The category is climate adaptation, which grew 64% to $5.5 billion in 2025 according to people who track that sort of thing. RIFFAI is small, early, probably six to twelve people, and almost certainly selling the same value proposition you walked into every ENI and NOPSEMA meeting with: the cost of not knowing is higher than the cost of the sensor. The difference is they are doing it in a regulatory environment that has already moved. Asia-Pacific governments buy climate adaptation intelligence because cyclones and fires are no longer theoretical. You built the KSAT monitoring business by identifying where pressure was accumulating and waiting for the explosion. RIFFAI is standing in the blast radius and calling it product-market fit. The hard part about joining an early-stage adaptation intelligence company is that the tech is genuinely difficult but the customers do not care about the tech. They care about the alert arriving before the flood does. If RIFFAI has figured out how to package hyperspectral detection as an insurance product or a compliance shortcut, that is the signal. If they are still explaining spectral bands to prospects, you will spend a year building the same pitch deck you built at KSAT in 2013. Either way, they are in a part of the market where mission and margin actually overlap, and Singapore as a hub means access to the entire Southeast Asian government and corporate buyer base without the friction of operating inside it.

watch_list.why: Satellite ML for powerline vegetation monitoring. Utility buyers. Proven traction.
watch_list.roast: Overstory uses machine learning to interpret satellite imagery and monitor vegetation near power lines, reducing wildfire risk and outages while cutting costs for utilities. The product works. The traction is real. PG&E, Xcel, and a dozen other grid operators are already paying them. It is one of the few climate adaptation plays where the buyer has an obvious budget line, a regulatory requirement, and a lawsuit-prevention motive all pointing at the same vendor. The problem for you is the buyer is electric utilities, and your PA Consulting credential is water and wastewater infrastructure, not grid operators. You assessed this gap in March when you looked at the Enterprise AE role and decided the fit was weaker than the job description suggested. That has not changed. What has changed is Overstory's commercial maturity. They are past the stage where they need someone to create the market. They need someone to scale an inside sales motion and close renewal cycles with utility procurement teams who think in three-year RFPs and rate case approvals. You sold oil spill monitoring to energy majors and fisheries surveillance to governments. The distance between your deal muscle and Overstory's current go-to-market is not skills, it is domain vocabulary, and that gap closes faster than people think. If they ever open a senior partnerships role focused on expanding outside North American utilities into international energy infrastructure or climate risk underwriters, that is a different conversation. Until then, this is a company to watch, not a company to join.

watch_list.why: UK climate tech using satellite/LiDAR/SAR for forest carbon baseline data. EUDR contract, carbon registry clients, direct MRV domain fit.
watch_list.roast: Space Intelligence is what happens when two guys measure 25,000 trees by hand in Africa, decide that was idiotic, and pivot to satellite LiDAR instead. Dr. Murray Collins and Professor Ed Mitchard launched this thing in 2017 after Mitchard earned a PhD figuring out you should not be walking around with a tape measure when you have radar. Now they sell forest carbon baselines to registries in Kenya, Tanzania, Argentina, and Indonesia, which means they are the people deciding whether your billion-dollar carbon credit is backed by actual trees or by aspirational PowerPoint. The firm just won the EU deforestation contract to help Italian Customs and Excise follow EUDR, which is the kind of obscure regulatory toehold that becomes a moat if the law sticks. You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill detection to people who did not want to buy it until the regulator made them. Space Intelligence is that same play, except the product is verifiable carbon data and the forcing function is EUDR, CDP, and every financial institution that just figured out nature risk is credit risk. They have drones, satellites, custom software, and the same problem GFW had, which is that being right about the science does not automatically make the commercial conversation easy. The difference is their clients are carbon credit developers, certification bodies, and governments who need someone to stand behind the baseline when the auditor shows up. You know how to build those relationships. You also know that the voluntary carbon market has a trust problem, and Space Intelligence is one of the few firms with peer-reviewed methodology and third-party validation, which is the only thing separating signal from noise right now. London-based, scaling hard, raising money, and looking for someone who understands how to translate satellite data into commercial traction with buyers who are skeptical by default. That is your entire professional life in one sentence.

watch_list.why: Indian EO startup launching world's first optical+SAR fusion satellite (OptoSAR). Defence, disaster, agriculture. Pre-revenue scaling phase, strong technical differentiation.
watch_list.roast: GalaxEye is building the world's first satellite that fuses optical and SAR into a single sensor package, which they are calling OptoSAR, a name that sounds like a pharmaceutical for orbital conjunctivitis but is actually a legitimately interesting technical bet. The thesis is simple. Optical gives you pretty pictures when the sun is out, SAR works at night and through clouds, and fusing them onboard means you get better change detection and faster answers without waiting for two different satellites to pass over the same target six hours apart. They are Bangalore-based, venture-backed, and about to launch their first demo satellite in 2026, which puts them in the same build-and-prove phase that Pixxel and Wyvern are navigating, except GalaxEye is focused on defence and disaster monitoring rather than hyperspectral agriculture. The market they are chasing is India, Southeast Asia, and Middle East governments who want sovereign EO capability but cannot afford to build Sentinel-2 themselves. You have sold to that buyer. You know the conversation. It starts with a pilot, moves to a service contract, stalls for eighteen months while procurement sorts out whether this is capital or operations budget, and closes when a minister sees a competitor using it. GalaxEye has the tech and the timing, but they are pre-revenue and figuring out how to package a novel sensor fusion capability for buyers who barely understand SAR by itself. This is the KSAT playbook all over again. Multi-mission value-added services on top of novel data sources, sold into markets where the regulatory or strategic pressure has not fully formed yet but is coming. The risk is they are early, the team is technical, and someone needs to build the commercial function from scratch. The opportunity is you have done this twice already, once in Tromso and once at GFW, and you know that the hardest part of selling EO is not the technology, it is finding the moment when the buyer can no longer afford to not know.

watch_list.why: Electrolyzer tech converting captured CO2 into e-methanol using renewable electricity. Modular, industrial emitter integration, emerging CCUS segment.
watch_list.roast: Oxylus Energy is doing electrolysis on captured CO2 to make e-methanol, which is either the future of decarbonized shipping fuel or a science fair project with a Series A, depending on whether you think maritime is serious about cleaning up. The technology is membranes, catalysts, and renewable electricity turning carbon dioxide into a liquid fuel you can drop into existing bunker infrastructure without ripping out every engine on the water. Modular carbon conversion factories, co-located with industrial emitters, turning waste gas into sellable product. It is a beautiful closed loop on paper. The commercial reality is harder. E-methanol costs more than fossil methanol today, the regulatory credit structures that would flip that equation are inconsistent across jurisdictions, and your customers are shipping companies and chemical producers who are extremely good at waiting until the regulation forces their hand. You know this buyer cold. You sold monitoring to oil majors who did not want it until NOPSEMA or the North Sea regulators made it a condition of the license. Oxylus is in the same position. The tech works, the use case is real, but the forcing function is still forming, and someone needs to go build the pilot-to-precedent engine that turns early adopters into regulatory reference points. They are early stage, heavily technical, and almost certainly underestimating how long it takes to move a maritime or chemical procurement cycle. But if you are looking for a company where the product is hard science, the market is strategic decarbonization, and the commercial model has to be invented rather than inherited, Oxylus is exactly that. The question is whether you want to spend three years explaining carbon intensity regulations to bunker fuel buyers, or whether you would rather sell satellite data to people who already know what a raster is. Both are hard. One plays to twenty years of EO and environmental markets. The other is a pivot into industrial decarbonization with all the pain that implies. Your call.

watch_list.category: Enhanced Rock Weathering / Carbon Removal MRV
watch_list.why: India-based, enhanced weathering with proprietary FELUDA measurement tech. Uses satellite monitoring, isotopic analysis, geochemical modeling. Emerging market, rigorous MRV, conservation angle with Darjeeling partner farms.
watch_list.roast: You named your carbon removal measurement technology FELUDA, which is either a Satyajit Ray reference or a very elaborate acronym, and you are grinding up silicate rock in Darjeeling and measuring soil chemistry to prove it actually pulls carbon out of the atmosphere. Enhanced rock weathering is the carbon removal method that sounds too simple to work, which means the entire commercial challenge is convincing people the measurement is not fake. You are using isotopic analysis, geochemical modeling, satellite imagery for vegetation monitoring, and life cycle assessments to build the MRV stack that makes the credits defensible. The problem is that nobody has figured out how to sell enhanced weathering carbon credits at scale yet, which means you are building the verification infrastructure before the market exists. Paul has spent fifteen years in that exact position. He sold proactive offshore monitoring before regulators required it, built a commercial licensing function on top of an open data platform before anyone agreed it was legitimate, and closed the Planet Labs deal by convincing a third party that high-latency conservation detection was not a competitive threat to their real-time intelligence product. If you are going to convert enhanced weathering from science project to commercial carbon removal product, you need someone who knows how to construct the market argument and the deal structure when the buyer does not know they are a buyer yet. That is the skill. You should use it.

watch_list.why: Munich-based, building their own IR nanosatellite constellation after proving the model on third-party data. Thermal imaging for real-time wildfire intelligence.
watch_list.roast: German thermal imaging satellite startup that's flying a constellation to detect wildfires early, which sounds like a no-brainer climate adaptation business until you try to sell real-time wildfire detection to a US Forest Service that's had its budget frozen since 2024 and a commercial forestry sector that already knows where the fires are by the time your satellite sees them. ESA is providing access to OroraTech's thermal data, which is great for credibility and terrible for revenue unless you've figured out how to convert a space agency partnership into paying customers. The tech works, the use case is real, the buyer is broke or already served, and the business model is the same problem every EO startup has: proving that space-based thermal beats the guy in the tower with binoculars and a radio. For a guy who sold KSAT's first commercial oil spill monitoring contract and then spent five years convincing Shell and ExxonMobil that proactive satellite surveillance was cheaper than reactive cleanup, you've already run this play. The pilots are easy. The MLA is hard. OroraTech has the pilots. The question is whether they can turn early detection into a recurring revenue contract with someone who has budget authority, or whether this becomes another technically successful EO company that can't figure out who pays.

watch_list.why: UK-based, builds SaaS tools for biodiversity, drought, wildfire, heat stress reporting using satellite data. Selling into corporate ESG and regulatory compliance channels.
watch_list.roast: Map Impact is what happens when someone realizes that companies do not buy climate data because they care about the climate, they buy it because their auditor told them to or because TCFD said so or because an underwriter is holding a renewal hostage. You have spent your whole career trying to make the virtue case for satellite data and Map Impact has accepted that virtue does not close. Compliance closes. Their tools have names like BiodiversityView and WildfireView, which is Product Marketing 101 for people who do not want to be in a meeting, and their pitch deck writes itself because half of Europe is already mandating this reporting and the other half will by 2027. The company is small, UK-based, probably eight to twelve people, and they need someone who knows how to take a working analytics engine and turn it into a channel partner play with the big four accounting firms and the mid-market ESG consultants. That is not a science problem. That is a KSAT problem. You did this once with oil majors who did not want to buy monitoring until NOPSEMA made them. You can do it again with corporates who do not want to buy biodiversity offsets until their CDP score depends on it. The work will feel like you are selling homework instead of hope, but the money is real and the exit market is every ag-tech or insurance-tech acquirer who needs a geospatial bolt-on to check a regulatory box.

watch_list.why: India-based spacetech building multi-spectral constellation combining thermal IR and visible imaging. Raised $2.2M seed in April 2026, targeting defense, agriculture, disaster response.
watch_list.roast: SatLeo Labs is building a thermal and visible imaging constellation out of India with a $2.2 million seed round and a pitch deck that says defense, agriculture, climate intelligence, and disaster response, which is startup-speak for we have not picked a customer yet but all of them sound good in a funding memo. They are early. The first satellite is not up. The team has good remote sensing credentials and backing from Unicorn India Ventures, which has actually returned money to LPs and knows how to pick deep tech that does not implode. The opportunity is that India is writing checks to build sovereign EO capacity and SatLeo is positioning as the domestic alternative to Planet, Maxar, and Airbus for Indian government and commercial buyers who want the data to stay in-country and the pricing to not require an act of parliament. You would be the first non-technical commercial hire walking into a team that has built satellites but never sold one. That is the KSAT job again, except this time you are doing it in Bangalore instead of Tromso, the customers speak English but operate on IST timelines, and your comp will probably include equity that could 10x if they actually deploy the constellation or could zero if thermal imaging turns out to be a feature not a platform. The India market is huge, underserved, and protectionist in exactly the way that favors a local startup with a good product over a foreign incumbent with a better one. If you want to build a commercial function from scratch in a market you have never worked, this is the call. If you want to stay within driving distance of a ski hill, this is not.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based MRV for smallholder farmers, profitable while competitors burn cash, 2M+ tonnes annually. Direct Planet/GFW analog in the carbon space.
watch_list.roast: Boomitra won the Earthshot Prize, got named to TIME's 100 Most Influential Companies, became profitable while competitors burned cash, and is now removing over two million tonnes annually across nine countries, which makes them one of the few soil carbon MRV platforms that is not a science project pretending to be a business. They use satellites and AI to cut MRV costs by 90 percent, which unlocks carbon finance for smallholder farmers in the Global South who have been locked out of voluntary markets by the cost structure of traditional verification. CEO Aadith Moorthy was recognized by TIME for prioritizing the communities most affected by climate change, and the company name means Friend of the Earth in Sanskrit, so they have the mission story nailed. They raised $4 million in Series A plus $35 million in project financing, and their backers include Chevron Technology Ventures, Jerry Yang, and Tom Steyer. You built GFW commercial licensing by finding the insurance and MRV buyers who needed a structured relationship on top of the free platform. Boomitra has the same structure except the free platform is the atmosphere and the structured relationship is a carbon credit with an ISOMETRIC or Verra stamp on it. The risk is that they are profitable today because they are serving the high-intent early adopters, and scaling to the next million hectares requires selling to buyers who are still not sure soil carbon is a real asset class. If voluntary markets unfreeze, Boomitra is the category leader. If they do not, even the profitable players run out of oxygen.

watch_list.why: Diversified carbon removal portfolio (regen ag, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced weathering), raised $20M Series B tranche Feb 2026, selling through Puro/Isometric/Verra. Commercial traction.
watch_list.roast: Paul, Varaha doesn't bet on one carbon pathway, which makes them either smart or hedged to the point of incoherence, depending on who's asking. They do regen ag, agroforestry, biochar, and enhanced rock weathering, selling verified credits through every major registry that will take their calls. It's the marketplace model GFW flirted with, except these guys are actually collecting the check at the end. They just closed the first $20M of a $45M Series B, which in today's carbon market is the equivalent of ExxonMobil returning your cold email in 2013. Based in India, backed by WestBridge. You've built commercial programs on top of open platforms, structured multi-party EO deals, and know exactly what it feels like to translate scientific credibility into a purchase order. This is that, but the science is soil models and the purchase order comes from corporate net-zero teams with compliance deadlines and Scope 3 anxiety. The commercial conversation is identical. The vertical is newer. The buyer has a gun to their head in the form of an earnings call. You've done harder.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based comprehensive MRV for agroforestry/reforestation combining satellites, field sensors, mobile apps. Tracks carbon + socioeconomic + biodiversity co-benefits. Ecosystem builder fit.
watch_list.roast: Paul, Treecycle is what happens when someone in Amsterdam realizes that carbon credits are worthless unless you can prove the trees are real, alive, and not about to get turned into firewood by a subsistence farmer three months after you sold the offset to KLM. Their MRV stack combines satellites, field sensors, and mobile apps for local project managers, which sounds like the KSAT multi-mission service pitch except the mission is not spilling oil, it's planting trees that stay planted. They also track socioeconomic and biodiversity co-benefits, which is either visionary or feature creep, but in this market it's the difference between a credit that sells and a credit that sits in inventory. You structured ASN's subsea cable partnership because you saw the non-obvious application. Agroforestry MRV is a non-obvious application of the same satellite monitoring discipline you've been selling for 20 years, except the regulator is the carbon registry and the anxious buyer is Unilever's supply chain VP. Amsterdam or remote. If you sold weekly GoM monitoring to Shell in The Hague for three years, you can sell continuous forest verification to European corporates with net-zero mandates. Same meetings, different NDA.

watch_list.category: AI-powered Satellite MRV for Carbon Credits
watch_list.why: India-based, AI + satellite imagery + blockchain MRV for nature-based carbon credits, processing remote sensing data through ML, exactly the Planet/GFW stack applied to carbon verification
watch_list.roast: Indian startup doing AI-powered MRV for nature-based carbon credits using satellite imagery, remote sensing, machine learning, and blockchain, which is either the future of carbon verification or four buzzwords in a trench coat pretending to be a business model. They process satellite data through ML algorithms and record verified emissions reductions on a blockchain ledger, which sounds impressive until you remember that the carbon market's credibility problem has never been about whether the data is on a blockchain. It's about whether the baseline was real and whether the forest would have been cut anyway. VANYA's pitch is high precision carbon stock measurements and transaction transparency through immutable records, which solves the auditing problem but not the additionality problem, and every carbon credit buyer you've ever met knows the difference. For a guy who negotiated the Planet Labs deal and then built the ASN subsea infrastructure partnership by identifying a non-obvious application for fisheries data, this is familiar territory. The tech stack is identical to what you used at GFW. The hard part is convincing a corporate buyer that satellite-derived carbon estimates are sufficient for a financial instrument their CFO has to defend. You've already done that conversation in the maritime insurance vertical. This is the same conversation in carbon, and you know which objections to surface in month two instead of month five.

watch_list.why: Israeli startup combining satellite data, climate models, and AI for agricultural supply chain resilience. Land-based value chains. Direct EO application with climate lens.
watch_list.roast: Tel Aviv outfit calling itself a copilot for resilient land-based value chains, which is consultant for we help farms not die when the weather goes sideways. They layer satellite data over climate models and call it AI insights, which is half the EO pitch decks you saw at KSAT and all the ones you will see if you take this job. The product is real, the use case is obvious, the market is every agribusiness pretending sustainable sourcing is more than a website paragraph. You have spent a decade making satellite data legible to buyers who do not want to become remote sensing experts. Marvin is selling the exact same translation problem in a different vertical. You know the NDVI is not the insight, the insight is knowing which supply chain manager has budget authority and why this quarter their bonus depends on proving they have visibility into upstream risk. You closed Planet at 95 percent off commercial rates by understanding who actually signs. Marvin will want someone who has done it before and who will not waste six months trying to sell the technology instead of the outcome. The risk is Israel-based in 2026, which complicates US federal and some commercial accounts, but if they are smart they have thought about that already. If they have not, you will spend the first 90 days explaining it.

watch_list.why: Extreme weather forecasting using radar and satellite. Climate adaptation tech, early warning systems. EO backbone with commercial and gov customers.
watch_list.roast: They are building better weather radar because the National Weather Service gear was installed when you were in middle school and extreme weather is now a weekly earnings call risk instead of an annual one. The pitch is speed and accuracy, which matters when you are a utility trying not to get sued or a city trying not to drown. Climavision has actual deployed systems, which in the climate adaptation space makes them the grown-ups. You spent the back half of KSAT building the marine plastics and IUU detection verticals, which was explaining to buyers why they should pay for something they did not know they needed until the regulatory pressure or the reputational risk made ignoring it more expensive than buying it. Weather intelligence is the same muscle. The buyer is not meteorology-curious, the buyer has a P&L problem that better forecasting solves. The company will want someone who understands the EO stack but sells the business outcome, which is the only thing you have ever done. The question is whether they have product-market fit at scale or whether this is still a services-heavy model pretending to be a platform. If it is the latter, you have seen that movie and you know it takes years to productize. If it is the former, this is the GFW-to-insurance vertical pattern you already ran and you would be dangerous in the first six months.

watch_list.why: Satellite imagery and ML to verify forest carbon projects. Nature-based carbon market infrastructure. YC-backed, major customers (Amazon, Airbnb, Netflix). Direct overlap with GFW Planet deal skillset and carbon market framing.
watch_list.roast: San Francisco forest carbon verification play that uses satellite imagery and machine learning to tell buyers whether the trees they paid for are actually standing or whether someone cut them down and planted corn. The entire nature-based carbon market only works if the credits are real, and Pachama has decided to be the referee, which is a terrible job with exceptional leverage. They have Amazon, Airbnb, and Netflix as customers, which means they have figured out how to sell to corporate sustainability teams with real budgets, and they are backed by Bill Gates and Chris Sacca, which means the cap table has more climate cred than most climate conferences. You built the Planet three-party deal and you spent two years explaining to insurance buyers and conservation funders why GFW-derived data products are not the same as the open-access platform. Pachama is the same structural tension packaged differently. The free satellite data is everywhere, the value is in the methodology, the validation, and the willingness to stake your reputation on the outputs. You know this pattern. You also know the carbon market is a minefield of credibility problems, which means Pachama spends half its time defending its methodology and the other half explaining why it is not just Verra with better marketing. If you go in, you will spend the first month learning why every objection you expect is wrong and the second month realizing some of them are not.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based dMRV for rice methane projects, Gold Standard pilot, ag carbon space aligns with Planet/GFW EO background
watch_list.roast: Paris-based outfit running the Gold Standard's first satellite-verified rice carbon project, which sounds impressive until you remember that rice paddies have been flooding and draining for five thousand years without anyone needing a Sentinel-2 feed and a machine learning model to confirm it happened. They're pitching digital MRV as the solution to scaling farmer-led climate finance, which is consultant for 'we will sell you software so you can spend less time arguing with auditors and more time explaining to a Bengali smallholder why this year's carbon credit payment is three months late because the cloud cover was bad in July.' The tech works. The pilot makes sense. The Gold Standard partnership is real. But you have seen this movie before at GFW, where the distance between a functioning MRV platform and a functioning revenue model is the length of the Tromso winter, and somebody still has to convince the buyer that the credit is worth more than the trouble. If they can make the unit economics work at paddy scale, they have built something no one else has. If they cannot, they have built a very expensive dashboard for an NGO that runs out of grant money in 2027. You would spend the first six months finding out which one it is, and you are extremely good at that.

watch_list.why: Boston-based, field spectroscopy for soil carbon, bridges EO/ground truth gap, solves expensive lab analysis problem in carbon markets
watch_list.roast: Boston outfit that decided the way to fix soil carbon MRV was to stop pretending you can do it all from space and just build a better shovel. Their proprietary probe uses spectroscopy to measure soil carbon content instantly in the field, no lab, no six-week turnaround, no $50 per sample cost structure that makes soil carbon projects uneconomical at any scale a farmer would recognize. It is a hardware play in a software-obsessed market, which means they either have something genuinely defensible or they have a very expensive field instrument that breaks when you drop it in Iowa mud. The thesis is sound: soil carbon is the problem child of carbon markets because measurement is brutal and satellite-based proxies are just fancy guesses with error bars nobody wants to admit in the methodology. If Yard Stick can cut MRV costs by two-thirds and make the data credible enough that Verra and Gold Standard accept it without a fight, they have removed the primary constraint on a multi-billion-dollar market that currently does not function. If they cannot, they have built a science project. You would spend the first three months figuring out whether their spectroscopy actually works across soil types or whether it only works in the test plots they show investors, and that is a question you are mean enough to ask in month one instead of month eighteen when the enterprise customer realizes the data does not match the lab and the contract goes sideways. They need someone who has sold technical credibility to skeptical buyers with sharp pencils, and you sold Shell a polar monitoring service they did not think they needed until you made the alternative feel riskier than writing the check.

watch_list.why: Satellite-powered digital MRV platform, third-party validated, farm/field/forest scale carbon monitoring, independent platform with no third-party incentives
watch_list.roast: CarbonSpace bills itself as independent and scientifically rigorous, which in the carbon MRV world means they watched Pachama and Sylvera raise nine figures and decided somebody needs to be the grownup in the room. Their pitch is peer-reviewed algorithms and zero perverse incentives, which is either refreshingly honest or a polite way of saying they have no idea how to monetize this yet. You spent a decade negotiating Planet imagery deals and ASN subsea cable partnerships where the carbon story was adjacent, not central. Here it is central, and the buyer is an ESG compliance officer at a reinsurer who needs audit-proof documentation before the CFO finds out what the premium increase looks like. CarbonSpace is betting the voluntary carbon market will grow a spine and demand real verification. You have spent your entire career finding the moment when a client realizes the status quo is riskier than the alternative. If that moment arrives for soil carbon the way it did for NOPSEMA offshore monitoring, CarbonSpace is the kind of platform that becomes the de facto standard. If it does not, they are a science project with a website. The question is whether you want to be in the room when that tipping point either happens or does not. Remote first, decent chance they need someone who has actually sold satellite-derived data products to someone other than a grant officer.

watch_list.why: US-built electric cargo vessels, MIT Ocean Engineering pedigree, $100M+ in sales, deploying early 2026, maritime innovation background, exactly Paul's domain
watch_list.roast: Voltic is building battery and solar powered cargo ships and claiming they are four times more profitable than diesel, which either means they found the naval architecture equivalent of cold fusion or their Excel model has some very optimistic assumptions about charging infrastructure in Philadelphia and retrofit timelines in Oakland. They spun out of MIT Ocean Engineering, raised money from the largest maritime VC in the world, and have over $100 million in signed sales, so somebody believes the physics work. The founding team previously started two shipping lines, which means they know how to move containers and also know how many ways a ship can lose money. You sold oil spill monitoring to Shell and ExxonMobil, you built the SkyTruth to GFW ecosystem over a decade, you closed the Planet Labs coastal monitoring deal, and you structured the ASN subsea cable partnership. Voltic is trying to electrify the dirtiest part of the global supply chain, and their early customers are either visionary or desperate. The commercial question is whether shipping companies will pay a green premium or whether this only works with IRA subsidies and California port incentives that disappear in 2027. If the unit economics are real, this is a category-defining company. If they are not, it is a very expensive science experiment that runs out of runway somewhere between the second ship and the third. Either way, you would be selling into the exact same maritime domain awareness and logistics buyers you have spent fifteen years cultivating, except this time the product is the ship instead of the imagery of the ship. That is either a natural adjacency or a costly distraction, depending on whether you want to sell hardware or data.

watch_list.why: Stuttgart-based, €5M seed March 2026, first satellite 2027. Methane monitoring from space with SWIR+micro-LIDAR at 2x accuracy vs existing systems. ESA-backed. Fits EO+climate+MRV commercial licensing experience perfectly.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil majors on proactive spill monitoring when they were pretending to care about their social license, and now here comes AIRMO out of Stuttgart with a rocket scientist founder and a satellite designed to catch methane leaks the energy industry has been hemorrhaging for profit. They raised €5 million in March from Ananda and kopa ventures to fly SWIR imaging with micro-LIDAR miniaturized onto a smallsat, which sounds like vaporware until you see ESA backing and airborne systems already flying commercial contracts in Europe and Central Asia. The pitch is that 70 percent of methane emissions go undetected, costing the energy sector $100 billion a year, $30 billion of that in straight revenue loss, which means there are CFOs at oil majors and pipeline operators who will pay real money if the data is defensible and the measurement is repeatable and you can walk them through how it works without making them feel stupid. That is the same conversation you had with Shell in The Hague in 2012 about why they should pay KSAT to watch their platforms when MDA had the incumbent contract. AIRMO is pre-revenue, probably six months from figuring out what enterprise sales actually means, and you have sold satellite-derived environmental monitoring to the exact verticals they are about to discover. The founder is technical, the investors are impact-first, and they will need someone who can explain to a procurement officer at TotalEnergies why continuous orbital monitoring creates regulatory and reputational value even when the law does not require it yet. Also they are in Germany, which you lived next door to for a decade, and they will absolutely botch the North American market entry unless someone who has actually closed energy contracts gets in early. This is the KSAT pitch all over again but with methane and the regulatory pressure is higher now than it was in 2015.

watch_list.why: California-based, $95M state contract signed March 2026 for real-time emissions enforcement. Satellite constellation detecting methane and CO2 point sources. Government and corporate licensing revenue model.
watch_list.roast: Carbon Mapper just signed a $95 million contract with the State of California for real-time emissions enforcement, which means they went from being a satellite company to being the enforcement arm of a regulatory regime, and that should make every oil and gas operator and landfill manager and wastewater facility in the state very nervous and very willing to pay for early access to the same data the state is using to write citations. You know this pattern. It is the pilot-to-precedent engine you ran at KSAT when you helped draft oil spill legislation in Nigeria and got KSAT language into AMSA RFPs in Australia. Carbon Mapper is flying purpose-built satellites to detect methane and CO2 point sources at facility scale, and the California contract proves the regulatory demand is real and someone is willing to pay government rates for continuous monitoring. The company is a partnership that spun out of JPL and university researchers, which means strong technical credibility and probably weak commercial instincts, and they are about to get flooded with inbound interest from every industrial emitter who wants to know what Carbon Mapper can see before the regulators do. They need someone who understands how to sell proactive monitoring to companies that do not want to be monitored, who can structure licensing deals with enterprises that have compliance and PR concerns baked into every conversation, and who knows how to price surveillance when the customer views it as insurance against regulatory penalties they have not budgeted for yet. The California contract is proof of concept for the regulatory market. The next $95 million comes from Chevron and Equinor and ConocoPhillips buying the same data stream under a commercial license so they can fix leaks before the state publishes them. You have sold this exact service. They need you six months ago.

watch_list.why: Announced Merlin constellation for daily one-meter multi-spectral remapping. Sub-daily revisit competing with Planet on coverage. Commercial imagery at scale.
watch_list.roast: Satellogic just announced Merlin, their new one-meter daily remap play, which means they finally looked at Planet's pricing and thought, we can do that cheaper. You spent a decade at KSAT selling monitoring contracts to people who needed proof the oil slick happened yesterday, not last week, so you know exactly what daily revisit unlocks and what it costs when the competitor launches faster than you close. Satellogic has been the plucky South American underdog since 2010, still trying to prove they belong in the room with Maxar and Planet, still pitching ten spectral bands like it is a personality. The good news is they are well funded, the constellation is real, and they have actual customers who pay. The bad news is commercial EO is a knife fight and you would be walking in as the partnerships guy tasked with making Merlin feel differentiated when everyone already has a Planet contract and a Maxar backup. But if you want to sell imagery where the margin lives in frequency not resolution, and you miss the days when you had to explain why a client should pay for monitoring they did not think they needed yet, this is that fight all over again. Just with more Slack and fewer oil majors.

watch_list.why: Operating HiVE constellation for land surface temperature. Agriculture, utilities, infrastructure use cases. Thermal data gap Planet and Maxar do not fill.
watch_list.roast: constellr is the German thermal infrared play that everyone in ag tech and utilities has heard about but nobody has actually bought yet, which is the story of every climate dataset until somebody's insurance premium doubles and suddenly thermal anomaly detection stops being a nice to have. You sold EO services into offshore energy when the business case was build it and maybe they will pay, so you understand the gap between a working sensor and a working revenue model. constellr has the HiVE constellation flying, they have NanoAvionics building the buses, and they are generating actual land surface temperature data that matters for crop stress, power grid resilience, and construction planning. The problem is thermal IR is expensive to build, hard to sell, and the customers who need it most are municipalities and agricultural co-ops who move at the speed of procurement hell. If you join, you are the first commercial hire walking into a team of German engineers who have spent five years perfecting the sensor and approximately eight minutes thinking about how to make a farmer in Iowa pay for it. But if you want to sell a dataset that genuinely does not exist anywhere else at scale, and you still remember what it felt like to walk into Shell in 2012 with a monitoring capability nobody asked for and walk out with a contract, constellr is that same bet with worse margins and better science.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based energy and environmental analytics. Just acquired by Energy Aspects for market intelligence integration. Methane, oil storage, shipping tracking.
watch_list.roast: Kayrros is the French satellite analytics firm that spent a decade building the best methane and energy infrastructure monitoring product nobody in oil and gas wanted to admit they needed, and then got acquired by Energy Aspects in March 2026 because apparently the only way to monetize climate data is to bundle it with the commodity intelligence the traders already pay for. You have spent your entire career trying to sell environmental monitoring to people who would rather not know, so you will recognize the Kayrros model immediately. They built a machine learning platform that detects methane plumes, tracks floating oil storage, and estimates production at refineries nobody wants monitored, all from satellite imagery they do not own, which is the right business model if you can survive long enough for regulation to make your product mandatory. The Energy Aspects acquisition is either a rescue or a victory lap depending on whether you think Kayrros was about to run out of runway or had already won. Either way, you would be walking into a post-acquisition integration where the French engineers who built the algos now report to London market analysts who think satellite data is a feature not a product. If you want to sell methane monitoring as a compliance tool in the two-year window before the EPA Endangerment Finding gets reinstated, and you do not mind that your beautiful environmental dataset is now a widget inside somebody else's commodity terminal, Kayrros is available. Just maybe check if they still have a partnerships function after the acquisition closed.

morning_briefs.notes: Daily / Morning Brief cron commit landed at 12:30 UTC but no morning_briefs entry was appended to dashboard-data.json (existing pipeline bug, last appended entry was 2026-05-01). This run filled the gap. Digest had 276 postings of which ~225 were aggregator-board URLs (Terra.do, Climatebase, Climate Change Jobs by city, Schmidt Marine listings, Esri portfolio, etc.) that link to listing pages rather than specific JDs and are unscoreable per rubric. After applying applications-dedup, watch_list checks, hard exclusions, title-level filtering for engineering/analyst/IC-SDR/recruiter roles, and the recently-ranked window, 8 specific JDs were dispatched to scoring subagents. Subagents could not fetch JDs from their sandboxed environment (WebFetch denied), so fetching was retried in main context. Sierra Club returned 403 (unreadable). 5 successful fetches plus 2 hard-exclusion title-checks plus 1 already-known-pattern (Bentley) produced 4 new Tier 3 dashboard entries: Bees & Bears Head of Risk Berlin (44.5), Bentley Principal Consultant Remote/GA (47.5), SF Estuary Institute Executive Director Richmond CA (52.0), Active Site Chief of Staff Cambridge MA (43.5). No Tier 1 or Tier 2 entries today, no deep dives triggered.

haiku:
Kyle Busch pneumonia
sepsis took the champion down
Thursday, age forty

Gabbard resigns DNI
husband's cancer changes all
effective June thirtieth

Garden Grove tank threat
thousands flee chemical spill
Orange County waits

Sox bullpen implodes
Twins eight, Boston six on Friday
Tolle's start was wasted

Iran framework talks
nuclear off the table first
Pakistan brokers

Colbert's Late Show ends
that staff photo went viral
bloated operation

World Court climate vote
one forty one nations back it
US voted no

Gas at record highs
Memorial Day road trips stall
Iran chokes supply

Red River's new guy
Max leaves tourism for The Hill
Mayfest approaches

Memorial Day weekend
Enchanted Circle honors dead
summer season kicks

Blue Planet III drops
David Attenborough sees
Planet data at work

White House gunfire
Secret Service returns shots
Trump safe in the house

Iran deal not close
Trump says time is on our side
Booker calls him fool

One forty one yes
eight no votes on climate law
US stands apart

Taos seniors sign
college letters, May nineteen
the gorge stays behind

CAS five hundred two
South Korea private launch
Svalbard hears first call

Redhead in Ranchos
drinks at Rolling Still someday
no buyers today

Monsoon not due yet
but lightning already here
rifle crack, checkpoint

Wheeler Peak holds snow
into June, the dashboard holds
zero ranked again

Psyche flew by Mars
forty five hundred klicks out
rock visits red rock

Kachina untracked
telemark in late May gone
memory remains

Data centers drink
coal smoke to think faster
the grid bends, not breaks

Rio Grande runs cold
snowmelt from the peaks above
the wire stays quiet

Trump links Iran deal
to Abraham Accords again
oil dropped five percent

UN climate vote
one forty-one say yes, eight no
US stands alone

Chemical tank leaks
Newsom begs Trump for money
Orange County waits

Sox swept at Fenway
twenty-two and twenty-nine
Mayer at short now

Super El Niño
predictors call it strongest
monsoon might arrive

Patriots Wednesday
kick in Seattle, September
Super Bowl revenge

Twins took three straight games
Brooks Lee's two-run single
wet Fenway, spring cold

Ice still on Wheeler
trucks roll through in May dust storms
telemark withdrawn

Red hair, freckled arms
across the table, first date
she orders whiskey

Dashboard refreshed twelve
times today, zero replies
haiku hold the line

Carbon MRV firms
promise satellite magic
pilots never scale

ATS black hole swallows
twenty years of deals closed hard
one keyword mismatch

Aurlandsdalen hut
Oskar graduates in June
Vilde waits, Jotunheimen

UN votes one forty
one to eight, Vanuatu leads
US stands alone

Super El Niño
coming, oceans warmest yet
the gorge runs lower

Memorial Day
Vietnam Wall, rifle salute
Taos rain on sage

Pueblo of Taos
liquor ordinance goes live
Federal Register

California tank
toxic chemical, fifty
thousand evacuate

Satellogic wants
one meter daily, Merlin
launches October

Ian McEwan says
pessimism is the problem
not the melting ice

Redhead on the wire
she ghosts, the dashboard refreshes
Orlandos at eight

Planet backlog shrinks
three party deal pays daily
Attenborough films it

The dashboard knows me
better than I know myself
haiku hold the line

Sox face Braves tonight
six forty-five first pitch, cheap
seats thirty-nine bucks

Paxton beats Cornyn
Trump's Texas running the whole
show now, Cruz smiles wide

Sox nine under five
Braves own best record Fenway
loss number nineteen

Oil below ninety
Iran deal draft floats, White House
rejects it by dawn

Pueblo liquor code
live yesterday after years
Federal Register

Kayrros split in two
Safran bought the defense side
energy sold off

Red Sox pitching holds
bats can't deliver the ninth
inning runner dies

Gorge wind through pinyon
May heat rising off the mesas
rainless for weeks now

Telemark in May
ski season memory fades
bike season opens

The dashboard refreshes
haiku rotate like seasons
no jobs, just waiting

Redhead at the brew
pub laughs, I miss the signal
back to the laptop

Sox face Chris Sale
Fenway four ten first pitch
one and six and one

Twins sweep Boston
soggy Fenway yields again
ten wins in fourteen

Liquor code goes live
Pueblo ordinance posted
May twenty six date

Wheeler Peak summer
snowmelt fills the Junction pools
pike rising at dusk

SpaceX sixty flights
Starship V3 waits its turn
sixty thousand sats

GOP redistricts
ten seats ahead for midterms
Trump at new poll lows

Prediction markets
addiction crisis brewing
twenty one years gone

Blue Planet Three
David Attenborough films
Planet small vessel
intros: No buyers today. Haiku hold the line.

Zero ranked. Maximum haiku.

The dashboard rests. The poet does not.

Between waves. A haiku to hold the space.
The mesa wind ran the screener today. Nothing made the cut except this.
The ATS ate them all. The haiku survived.
Trump signed the deep-sea mining order Friday. ISA gets bypassed, the seabed gets sold by decree. Pipeline ran quiet anyway. Haiku held.
MethaneSAT went dark in orbit. CBAM hit seventy-five a ton. Your old world keeps shifting. Pipeline quiet. Haiku shows up.
Brussels punted EUDR another year. The pipeline did not blink. Haiku holds.
Tanager-1 caught ten methane leaks for California's air board. Microsoft buys ninety percent of the carbon market by itself now. Pipeline quiet. Haiku stretches.
Virginia redrew four House seats in a single referendum. Pipeline did not flinch. Haiku keeps count.
Cora got fired Saturday night, ten and seventeen out of the gate. Tracy ran the bench Sunday and won five to three behind a Contreras homer. Pipeline ran quiet. Haiku showed up.
Sangres east at one percent of normal snowpack. Palo Flechado at four. Acequias rationing in Talpa already. The water year is the story now. Pipeline quiet. Haiku holds.
EDF and fifteen co-petitioners filed against the EPA endangerment repeal April sixteenth. The litigation calendar is the work now. Pipeline quiet. Haiku on the wall.
Patriots took Caleb Lomu in the first round. Trench got an adult. Pipeline did not. Haiku covers the gap.
Artemis 2 came back from the moon April first, four humans, fifty years between. Pipeline quiet that week too. Haiku still here.
